lessons.lessonTitle: Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation
objectives: This lesson aims to introduce the students to the
life of the nomadic people and how traditional
elements of their heritage continue to feature
prominently in the current social life of the UAE.
The lesson underlines the idea of community
cooperation stemming from the necessity to survive
in face of the physical environment in which the
nomadic community lived. The tradition of communal
consensus and sharing of resources paved the way for
the formation of the UAE.

lessons.lessonTitle: The Consultative Governance in the UAE
objectives: The aim of the lesson is to introduce the fact that the
traditional political heritage of the UAE still continues
in the modern state. It is based on the principles of
Shura, an Islamic method of decision making, where
counsel and advice is sought from those who are
affected by the decision to be taken.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies Unit 1 Ethics in the Context of Communities Federal National Council: Identify the key developments in the growth of consultative government at local and national Discuss the ways and extent to which the UAE’s political heritage, based on consensus, continues to be part of the modern state. Engage in debate in ways which demonstrate an understanding of, and willingness to participate in, processes of consultation aimed at achieving Read the quote below. How can you link it to the following terms associated with UAE government and decision-making? The Federal National Council “Our system of government is based on our religion, and is what our people want. Should they seek alternatives, we are ready to listen to them. We are all in the same boat, and they are both captain and crew.” Sheikh Zayed Bin Sultan Al Nahyan - May God Bless His Soul The aim of the lesson is to introduce the fact that the traditional political heritage of the UAE still continues in the modern state. It is based on the principles of Shura, an Islamic method of decision making, where counsel and advice is sought from those who are affected by the decision to be taken. objectives: Trace the origins of the Ottoman Empire from Central Asian Muslim Turkoman tribes to its founding by Uthman I in 1299
Explain the significance of the Battle of Manzikert (1071) as a Seljuk victory that opened Anatolia to Islam
Describe the conquest of Constantinople (1453) by Sultan Muhammad al-Fatih and explain its importance as the fulfillment of a Prophetic glad tiding
Identify Sultan Selim I's conquests of the Arab lands (1516–1517) and explain why the title 'Khadim al-Haramayn al-Sharifayn' was granted
Create a historical timeline of key Ottoman events using digital tools
lessons.content: The Ottoman Empire was one of the greatest and longest-lasting empires in history. It lasted for over six centuries, from the year 1299 until 1922. At its height, the empire stretched across a vast area that included what is today Turkey, the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of Eastern Europe. The Ottomans were a Muslim empire, and their sultans carried the title of Caliph, which meant they were the political and spiritual leaders of the Muslim world. The empire was incredibly wealthy and powerful. Its economy was built on many things: trade along the old Silk Road routes, farming of wheat, barley, cotton, olives, and fruits, as well as crafts such as ceramics, textiles, and metalwork. Their position between Europe, Asia, and Africa made them a central hub of world trade for centuries. The story of the Ottomans begins with Muslim Turkish-speaking tribes from Central Asia. Over many centuries, these tribes gradually moved westward. They had already embraced Islam long before they reached the lands of Anatolia (modern-day Turkey). A very important event took place in the year 1071, when the Great Seljuk Empire, led by Sultan Alp Arslan, defeated the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Manzikert. This great victory opened the doors of Anatolia for Muslim settlement. After this battle, many Muslim Turkoman tribes migrated into the region and made it their home. It is important to note that the Ottomans did not yet exist at this time; the Battle of Manzikert was a Seljuk victory, not an Ottoman one. In the year 1299, a Muslim leader named Uthman I (known as Uthman Ghazi, meaning one who strives in the path of Allah) united a group of Turkoman tribes and established a new state in north-western Anatolia. The state was named after him: the word 'Ottoman' comes from the name 'Uthman.' From these humble beginnings, a small principality grew into one of the greatest empires the world has ever known. One of the most important events in Islamic history took place in 1453. Sultan Muhammad al-Fatih (Mehmed II) led the Ottoman army to conquer Constantinople, which had been the capital of the Byzantine Empire for over a thousand years. This conquest was deeply significant for Muslims because the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) had given a glad tiding about this event when he said: 'You shall conquer Constantinople. Blessed is its commander and blessed is its army.' After the conquest, the city became the new capital. The Ottomans showed great respect to its people, allowing Christians and Jews to continue practising their religions under the protection of the Islamic state. In 1516, Sultan Selim I (Yavuz Sultan Selim) turned the Ottoman army southward toward the Arab lands. At that time, Syria, Palestine, Egypt, and the Hijaz were ruled by the Mamluk Sultanate. The two armies met at the Battle of Marj Dabiq in northern Syria in 1516, where the Ottomans, by the will of Allah, defeated the Mamluk forces. The following year, in 1517, the Ottomans conquered Egypt. Through these conquests, the Ottomans gained control over Makkah and Madinah. The last Abbasid Caliph in Cairo formally handed over the title of Caliph to Sultan Selim I. From that point onward, the Ottoman sultans became known as the Caliphs of Islam and were given the title 'Khadim al-Haramayn al-Sharifayn' (Servant of the Two Noble Sanctuaries). The sacred relics of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) were brought from Cairo to Istanbul, where they remain preserved to this day. Later, under Sultan Sulayman the Magnificent (Sulayman al-Qanuni, who became sultan in 1520), the empire reached its greatest heights. He expanded Ottoman rule deeper into Iraq, Hungary, and North Africa, and commissioned beautiful mosques and public buildings.
activities: Activity 1: Build a Digital Timeline (MS Teams Assignment) — Create a timeline of at least 5 key events from this lesson using Canva, PowerPoint, or any digital tool. Include the date, event name, and a brief description for each event. Submit as file upload on MS Teams.

Activity 2: Explore Historical Maps (oldmapsonline.org) — Go to https://www.oldmapsonline.org and search for 'Ottoman Empire.' Find a historical map showing the empire around 1500. Take a screenshot. lessons.lessonTitle: Managing the Empire & Rivalry with Europe
objectives: Explain how the Ottomans governed their empire through the province system and the role of Beys
Describe the role and significance of the Janissaries as elite soldiers in the Ottoman military
Analyze why the Ottomans built a strong navy and its importance in protecting Muslim lands and trade routes
Compare and contrast the Ottoman land-based expansion with European sea-based expansion using maps and written analysis
Evaluate the significance of the Ottoman siege of Vienna (1529) for both the Ottoman Empire and Europe
lessons.content: After conquering Syria, the Balkans, Iraq, and Egypt, the Ottomans faced a big challenge: how do you govern such a huge and diverse empire? The answer was to build a well-organised system of administration. The Ottomans were very practical and wise in how they managed their new territories. They divided the empire into provinces, each overseen by an appointed governor called a Bey (or sometimes a Pasha for larger provinces). These governors were responsible for collecting taxes, maintaining security, and ensuring that justice was upheld according to Islamic law. In many of the newly conquered Arab lands, the Ottomans kept local leaders and rulers in place. This was a very smart approach: instead of removing everyone and putting strangers in charge, they allowed local governors, tribal leaders, and community elders to keep their positions, as long as they remained loyal to the Ottoman sultan and paid their taxes on time. This helped the local people feel that their lives and customs were respected, and it made the transition much smoother. Many Ottoman soldiers and administrators who settled in these provinces married local women, which created strong family ties between the Ottoman centre and the distant provinces. The Ottoman Empire was one of the first states to use a system of trained professional administrators across such a vast territory. The governors (Beys) had to report regularly to the central government in Istanbul, and inspectors were sent to check that everything was running fairly. This system helped keep the empire together for over 600 years! One of the most famous and unique features of the Ottoman military was the Janissary corps (in Turkish: Yeniçeri, meaning 'new soldier'). The Janissaries were an elite group of soldiers who served as the personal guard of the Ottoman sultan. They were among the first soldiers in the world to be trained in the use of firearms, and they became famous for their discipline, loyalty, and effectiveness in battle. The Janissaries were known for their distinctive uniforms, which included tall white felt hats and long moustaches. They also had their own marching bands (mehter) with drums, cymbals, and horns. Ottoman military music was so impressive that European armies later copied the idea of having military bands! The Mehter band is still active in Turkey today and performs at special ceremonies. The Ottomans did not only have a powerful army; they also built a strong navy. This was very important because the empire controlled long coastlines along the Mediterranean Sea, the Red Sea, the Black Sea, and the Arabian Gulf. The Ottoman navy protected Muslim traders, pilgrims travelling to Makkah and Madinah, and coastal cities from pirates and enemy fleets. In the early 1500s, a new challenge appeared. The Portuguese, who had sailed around Africa to reach the Indian Ocean, began attacking and raiding the coasts of the Arabian Gulf and the Arabian Peninsula. They threatened the trade routes that connected the Muslim world to India and Southeast Asia, and they endangered the safety of Muslim pilgrims travelling by sea to the holy cities. The Ottomans responded by sending a powerful fleet to the region in the 1550s, led by the famous admiral Piri Reis. Admiral Piri Reis was not only a skilled sailor but also a famous mapmaker. In 1513, he drew one of the oldest surviving maps of the world, which included surprisingly accurate details of the coastlines of Africa, Europe, and even parts of the Americas. By 1529, less than a century after the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, the Ottoman army had marched all the way to the city of Vienna, the capital of Austria, in the heart of Europe. The Ottomans laid siege to Vienna. Although they eventually withdrew, their arrival at the gates of Vienna sent a powerful message to all of Europe: the Ottoman Empire was a major world superpower. There was a very important difference between how the Ottoman Empire expanded and how the Western European empires expanded. The Ottoman Empire grew by expanding over land. All of its territories were connected by land. The Ottomans built roads, bridges, caravanserais, and fortresses across their vast territories. This land-based empire was similar to how earlier great Islamic empires had expanded, such as the Umayyad and Abbasid empires. The Western European empires, on the other hand, grew by sailing across the seas. Starting in the late 1400s and 1500s, countries like Portugal, Spain, Britain, and the Netherlands sent ships across the Atlantic Ocean to establish colonies in the Americas, Africa, and Asia. These colonies were far away from Europe, separated by vast oceans, and were controlled through sea routes and naval power.
activities: Activity 1: Venn Diagram — Land vs. Sea Empires (MS Teams Assignment) — Create a digital Venn diagram comparing the Ottoman Empire's land-based expansion with the European sea-based expansion. Include at least 4 points in each section. Submit via MS Teams.

objectives: Explain who Sultan Suleyman I was and why he is considered the Ottoman Empire's greatest ruler
Describe the meaning of his title 'Qanuni' (the Lawgiver) and distinguish between divine law (Sharia) and its human codification
Trace Suleyman's major conquests including Iraq, the Siege of Vienna, and North Africa using maps and timelines
Create a historical timeline of key events during Suleyman's reign using digital tools
lessons.content: Sultan Suleyman I, who ruled from 1520 to 1566, is widely regarded as the Ottoman Empire's greatest and most accomplished sultan. He reigned for nearly half a century, a period that became known as the golden age of Ottoman civilisation. During his rule, the empire reached the peak of its political, military, and cultural power, stretching across three continents and influencing the course of world history. His contemporaries in Europe called him 'Suleyman the Magnificent' because of the wealth, splendour, and power of his court, but to his own people he was known by a far more meaningful title: Qanuni, meaning 'the Lawgiver.' Why was he called 'Qanuni'? This title requires careful understanding. In Islamic theology, Allah alone is the Shari' (the Lawgiver). Suleyman's title Qanuni did not mean that he created or invented laws. Rather, it meant that he systematised and codified the existing Law (Sharia) — organising the rulings of jurisprudence into coherent legal codes known as qanun-name. He also promulgated secular administrative regulations ('urfi law) governing taxation, land tenure, and criminal penalties. This distinction between divine law and its human codification is central to the Islamic understanding of governance, and Suleyman's achievement was to bring order, consistency, and justice to the application of God's law across a vast and diverse empire. After his father, Sultan Selim I, conquered Egypt in 1517, the Ottoman sultans adopted the title of Caliph (Khalifah) — claiming leadership over the entire Muslim world. Suleyman ruled not just as a sultan but as Caliph of the Islamic world, and this shaped his self-understanding as a guardian of Islamic law. Suleyman expanded his territory through both naval and army conquests across the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe. One of his earliest and most significant victories was the Battle of Mohacs in 1526, where the Ottoman army decisively defeated the Kingdom of Hungary. This victory opened much of Central Europe to Ottoman influence and led to the partition of Hungarian lands. Following this triumph, Suleyman's forces pushed further westward, and in 1529 he laid siege to the Austrian city of Vienna. Although the siege was ultimately unsuccessful, the very fact that the Ottoman army had reached the gates of Vienna sent shockwaves across Europe. In 1534, Suleyman turned his attention eastward and conquered Iraq from the Safavid Empire. The conquest of Iraq gave the Ottomans access to the Arabian Gulf, where they established trade routes and engaged in naval battles with the Portuguese along the Indian coast. During Suleyman's reign, Ottoman influence in North Africa also grew. Algiers was not really 'conquered' by Suleyman. The famous privateer Hayreddin Barbarossa had already established control in Algiers and voluntarily offered it to the Ottomans in 1519–1520, during the reign of Selim I. Suleyman later appointed Barbarossa as Grand Admiral (Kapudan Pasha) of the Ottoman fleet, which strengthened Ottoman naval dominance across the Mediterranean. Tripoli (modern Libya) was conquered in 1551. It is essential to understand that some of the territories associated with Suleyman's reign were actually conquered by his father, Sultan Selim I, who ruled from 1512 to 1520. Selim I conquered Syria (1516), Egypt (1517), and the Hejaz (1517), including the holy cities of Makkah and Madinah. Suleyman inherited these rich and strategically vital lands. By the time of Suleyman's death in 1566, the Ottoman Empire had been transformed into a major world power.

Activity 2: Explore Historical Maps — Find a historical map showing the Ottoman Empire around 1550 (during Suleyman's reign) on oldmapsonline.org. Compare with modern Google Maps. Which modern-day countries were once part of the Ottoman Empire under Suleyman? Write 3–4 sentences. Submit via MS Teams.

objectives: Describe the transformation of Constantinople into Istanbul and explain its significance for the Ottoman Empire
Identify the key features of the Suleymaniye Mosque Complex and explain how it served as a community centre for the Ottoman people
Explain Mimar Sinan's architectural innovations and how they differed from European architectural traditions
Compare and contrast Ottoman and European architectural styles using the Suleymaniye Mosque and St. Peter's Basilica as examples
Analyse the importance of public institutions (hospitals, kitchens, schools) in Ottoman society and their relevance today
lessons.content: In 1453, Sultan Muhammad al-Fatih (Mehmed II) conquered Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire for over a thousand years. This conquest fulfilled the glad tiding of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). After the conquest, the city became the new capital of the Ottoman Empire. The Ottomans renamed it Istanbul, a name derived from the Greek phrase 'eis ten polin' meaning 'to the city' or 'in the city.' Over time, Istanbul became one of the greatest cities in the world — a vibrant, cosmopolitan centre where Muslims, Christians, and Jews lived together under the protection of the Islamic state. The early Ottoman state had consolidated its power around Anatolia and the Balkans, with the Sea of Marmara as the critical geographic axis. Key early Ottoman capitals — Sogut, Bursa, and Edirne — were all in the Marmara-Balkan corridor. After the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Istanbul became the permanent capital, sitting at the crossroads of Europe and Asia, controlling the Bosphorus Strait. The Suleymaniye Complex (Suleymaniye Kulliyesi) is a multi-functional complex founded by Sultan Suleyman I in Istanbul. It is considered one of the greatest architectural masterpieces by the legendary Ottoman architect Mimar Sinan. The building was constructed between 1550 and 1559. It is a complex of multiple buildings that give insight into the Ottoman way of creating institutions for the public to gather for prayer, education, and medical care. Rather than simply building a mosque, Sultan Suleyman created a kind of community centre that served the holistic needs of the people. The complex included: a grand mosque for congregational prayer; four colleges (madrasas) for the study of Islamic law, one for each of the four Sunni schools of jurisprudence — the Hanafi, Shafi'i, Maliki, and Hanbali schools; a medical school; a hospital (dar al-shifa) that provided free healthcare to all; a public kitchen (imaret) that served free meals to the poor, students, and travellers; public baths (hammam) for hygiene and community wellbeing; a hospice or informal hotel (tabhane) for visitors and travellers; and a library. Mimar Sinan (c. 1488–1588) is widely regarded as the greatest architect of the Ottoman Empire and one of the most brilliant engineers in world history. He served as the Chief Architect under three sultans: Suleyman I, Selim II, and Murad III. Over his long career, he designed and supervised the construction of over 300 structures. One of Sinan's greatest achievements was his ability to support massive domes through internal structural systems — using half-domes, massive piers, thick walls, and concealed buttressing within the building's mass. He did NOT use external flying buttresses. Flying buttresses are a feature of Gothic European architecture (such as Notre-Dame de Paris), where external stone arches prop up the walls from the outside. Sinan's genius was precisely in avoiding the need for those external 'skeletal' supports. His domes appear to float effortlessly above vast, open interior spaces, with all the structural engineering hidden within the building's elegant form. The Suleymaniye Mosque and St. Peter's Basilica were both grand religious complexes built around the same time period with large central domes, but they belonged to fundamentally different architectural traditions that reflected different civilisations, different religious practices, and different approaches to structural engineering. The Suleymaniye emphasises open interior space, geometric harmony, and the integration of public services within a religious complex. St. Peter's emphasises monumental scale, figural art, and the authority of the Church through artistic grandeur. Other works by Mimar Sinan include the Sehzade Mosque (built for Suleyman's beloved son who died young), the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne (which Sinan considered his masterpiece), and the Mihrimah Sultan Mosque in Istanbul. The Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque) was designed by Sedefkar Mehmed Agha, a student of Mimar Sinan, and built between 1609–1617.
activities: Activity 1: Compare and Contrast — Venn Diagram — Create a digital Venn diagram comparing the Suleymaniye Mosque and St. Peter's Basilica. Include at least 3 points in each section. Focus on architectural features, purpose, and cultural significance. Submit via MS Teams.

Activity 2: Virtual Mosque Tour — Search online for a virtual tour of the Suleymaniye Mosque. Write down: (a) 3 architectural features you can identify; (b) 2 things that surprise you; (c) 1 question you still have. Submit notes via MS Teams.

Activity 3: Fill in the Blanks — Complete the paragraph using words from the word bank: Istanbul | Constantinople | Sinan | Suleyman I | Empire | provinces | Anatolia and the Balkans | Suleymaniye Complex. Submit via MS Teams.

Activity 5: Heritage Preservation Discussion — On MS Teams discussion board, respond in at least 5 sentences: 'The Suleymaniye Complex is over 460 years old and still stands in Istanbul. Why is it important to preserve historical buildings like this? What can we learn from them?'
